content.text: Mitochondria generate most of the cell's ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. They have a double membrane — the inner membrane is highly folded into cristae, maximizing surface area for the electron transport chain. Fun fact: they have their own DNA, supporting the endosymbiotic theory.

points: Prophase: chromosomes condense, spindle fibers begin to form
Metaphase: chromosomes align at the cell's equator (metaphase plate)
Anaphase: sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles
Telophase: nuclear envelopes reform, chromosomes decondense
Cytokinesis: cytoplasm divides to produce two daughter cells

subject: History
title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall

content.text: On November 9, 1989, the Berlin Wall fell after 28 years of dividing East and West Berlin. Growing pressure from peaceful protests and political reform in Eastern Europe led the East German government to open the border. It became the symbol of the end of the Cold War and led to German reunification in 1990.

content.question: What is the quadratic formula used to solve ax² + bx + c = 0?
content.answer: x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) / 2a. The discriminant (b² - 4ac) determines the number of solutions: positive = 2 real, zero = 1 real, negative = 2 complex.

content.question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?

content.text: DNA replication is semi-conservative — each new double helix contains one original and one new strand. Helicase unwinds the helix, primase adds RNA primers, and DNA polymerase III synthesizes the new strand in the 5'→3' direction. The leading strand is continuous; the lagging strand is built in Okazaki fragments.

points: Militarism: European nations built up massive armies and navies
Alliances: complex web of treaties meant one conflict could drag in many nations
Imperialism: competition for colonies created international tensions
Nationalism: ethnic pride fueled rivalries, especially in the Balkans
Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand was the immediate trigger

content.text: In any right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides: a² + b² = c². This is one of the most fundamental relationships in geometry, used in distance calculations, trigonometry, and even 3D space.

points: 200 OK — request succeeded
301 Moved Permanently — resource has a new URL
404 Not Found — server can't find the resource
500 Internal Server Error — server encountered an unexpected condition

content.question: Which organelle is responsible for protein synthesis?
When and where did the Renaissance begin, and what was its core idea?
content.answer: The Renaissance began in 14th-century Italy (Florence). Its core idea was humanism — a renewed focus on human potential, classical learning, art, and science, marking the transition from the medieval to the modern world.

content.question: What is the derivative of f(x) = x³ + 2x?

content.text: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules that lets different software applications communicate. Think of it as a waiter in a restaurant: you (the client) tell the waiter (API) what you want, and they bring it from the kitchen (server). REST APIs use HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.

content.question: What is the overall chemical equation for photosynthesis?
content.answer: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Carbon dioxide and water are converted into glucose and oxygen using sunlight, primarily in the chloroplasts.

content.text: The French Revolution (1789–1799) overthrew the monarchy and established France as a republic. Triggered by economic crisis, social inequality, and Enlightenment ideas, key events included the storming of the Bastille, the Declaration of the Rights of Man, and the Reign of Terror. It fundamentally changed European political systems.

Balanced search trees guarantee logarithmic performance for all operations, unlike regular BSTs which can have poor worst-case performance. They slightly relax perfect balance requirements to maintain guaranteed ~lg N search time for N nodes, making them ideal for dynamic insertions while preserving efficiency.

points: 2-nodes hold one key and two links (left for smaller keys, right for larger keys)
3-nodes hold two keys and three links (left, middle, and right subtrees)
All null links are the same distance from root (perfectly balanced)
Nodes can hold multiple keys unlike standard BST nodes

content.text: Searching in a 2-3 tree works like BST search but compares against multiple keys per node. Compare the search key against all keys at current node - if found, it's a hit. Otherwise, follow the appropriate link based on which interval contains the search key until reaching a null link (miss) or finding the key.

points: Inserting into 2-node: Simply replace with 3-node containing both keys
Inserting into 3-node: Create temporary 4-node then split it
4-node splits into two 2-nodes with middle key moving up to parent
Process maintains perfect balance throughout the tree

content.question: What happens when you need to split a 4-node at the root of a 2-3 tree?
content.answer: The 4-node splits into three 2-nodes, with the middle key becoming the new root, increasing the tree height by 1. This is the only way 2-3 trees grow taller.

content.text: All 2-3 tree insertions involve purely local transformations - only the specific nodes and links being modified need to be examined. No other part of the tree is affected, and each transformation involves changing only a bounded constant number of links while preserving global order and balance properties.

points: Unlike regular BSTs that grow down from top, 2-3 trees grow up from bottom
Height increases only when root splits (rare occurrence)
All paths from root to null links maintain same length
Growth is perfectly balanced - no path becomes longer than others

content.question: When inserting into a 3-node whose parent is also a 3-node, what happens?
options: Create 4-node, split it, then create another 4-node in parent

content.text: 2-3 trees maintain perfect balance by ensuring all null links are equidistant from the root. Every local transformation during insertion preserves this property - paths remain the same length except when the root splits, which increases all path lengths by exactly 1.

The dynamic connectivity problem involves determining whether two objects are connected through a series of relationships. Given pairs of objects that are connected, we need to efficiently answer whether any two objects belong to the same connected component without storing all possible connection paths.

points: Networks: Determining if computers can communicate through existing connections
Variable-name equivalence: Checking if two variable names refer to the same object in programming
Mathematical sets: Testing if two integers belong to the same set
Social networks: Finding if people are connected through friendships

content.text: The 'is connected to' relationship is an equivalence relation with three key properties: reflexive (p connects to p), symmetric (if p connects to q, then q connects to p), and transitive (if p connects to q and q connects to r, then p connects to r). This partitions objects into equivalence classes called connected components.

points: union(p, q): Add connection between sites p and q
find(p): Get component identifier for site p
connected(p, q): Check if p and q are in same component
count(): Return total number of components

content.question: What happens to the number of components when union() merges two different components?
content.answer: The number of components decreases by 1, since two separate components become one.

content.text: Union-Find uses a site-indexed array id[] where each position represents a site (0 to N-1) and stores component information. Initially, each site is its own component with id[i] = i, and we use one site's name as the identifier for each component.

points: Good algorithms can make impossible problems solvable
Efficient algorithms can be as simple to code as inefficient ones
Understanding performance characteristics is intellectually rewarding
Scientific method helps choose between different solutions
Iterative refinement leads to increasingly efficient algorithms

content.text: To analyze Union-Find algorithms, we count array accesses (reads and writes) as our primary performance metric. This provides a useful way to compare different implementations since running time is typically within a constant factor of array access count.

content.question: Which property ensures that if A connects to B and B connects to C, then A connects to C?

content.text: The Union-Find algorithm filters out redundant connections by only outputting pairs p-q when they're not already connected through existing relationships. If previous pairs already imply that p and q are connected, the new pair is ignored.

Big O notation describes how an algorithm's running time grows as input size increases. It focuses on the dominant term and ignores constants - for example, 3n² + 100n becomes O(n²) because n² grows fastest for large inputs.

points: O-notation: Upper bound - algorithm runs no slower than this
Ω-notation: Lower bound - algorithm runs no faster than this
Θ-notation: Tight bound - algorithm runs exactly at this rate
All three describe algorithm efficiency for large input sizes

content.text: Algorithm growth rates from fastest to slowest: constant O(1), logarithmic O(log n), linear O(n), linearithmic O(n log n), quadratic O(n²), cubic O(n³), exponential O(2ⁿ). Each step represents a significant performance difference.

points: Single loop 1 to n: O(n) time
Nested loops both 1 to n: O(n²) time
Loop that doubles counter (i = 2*i): O(log n) time
Combine multiplicatively for nested structures

content.question: If an algorithm runs in O(n³) time, how much slower is it when input size doubles?
content.answer: 8 times slower. When n becomes 2n, the time becomes (2n)³ = 8n³, which is 8 times the original n³.

content.text: Standard merge sort splits arrays into 2 parts, but you can generalize to k-way splits. 3-merge sort splits into 3 parts, and all variants maintain O(n log n) time complexity because the tree depth changes but total work per level stays linear.

points: Find contiguous subarray with largest sum in an array
Brute force: O(n³) - check all possible subarrays
Improved: O(n²) using prefix sums for fast range queries
Optimal: O(n) using dynamic programming (Kadane's algorithm)
Divide & conquer: O(n log n) splitting problem in half

content.question: Which function grows fastest asymptotically?

content.text: The logarithm of n factorial, log(n!), equals Θ(n log n). This is because n! ≈ (n/e)ⁿ√(2πn) by Stirling's approximation, so log(n!) grows at the same rate as n log n asymptotically.

points: Drop constants: 5n² becomes O(n²)
Keep highest degree term: n³ + n² becomes O(n³)
Logarithm bases don't matter: log₂ n = O(log n)
max(f(n), g(n)) = Θ(f(n) + g(n)) for non-negative functions

content.question: What's the difference between Θ(n²) and O(n²)?
content.answer: Θ(n²) means exactly quadratic growth (tight bound), while O(n²) means at most quadratic growth (upper bound). An O(n) algorithm is also O(n²), but not Θ(n²).

content.question: Is 2^(n+1) = O(2^n)?
options: No, because the exponent is larger
Only for small values of n
Depends on the base of the logarithm

content.text: Asymptotic notation describes how algorithms perform as input size grows. Big O (O) gives upper bounds, Theta (Θ) gives tight bounds, and Omega (Ω) gives lower bounds. This helps compare algorithm efficiency without getting lost in implementation details.

points: Drop constants: 5n² becomes O(n²)
Keep highest degree term: n² + n becomes O(n²)
Logarithm bases don't matter: log₂n = O(log n)
Exponentials grow faster than polynomials: 2ⁿ > n³

content.text: To analyze loop running time, count how many times each operation executes. For nested loops, multiply the iterations. If a loop variable doubles each time (i = 2·i), it runs O(log n) times since you can only double log n times before exceeding n.

content.question: If an algorithm runs in 7n³ time, how much slower is it when input size doubles?
content.answer: 8 times slower. When n becomes 2n, the time becomes 7(2n)³ = 7·8n³ = 8 times the original time.

points: O(1) - Constant time, best case
O(log n) - Logarithmic, very efficient
O(n) - Linear time, scales directly
O(n log n) - Efficient sorting algorithms
O(n²) - Quadratic, nested loops

content.text: Standard merge sort splits arrays in half (2-way), but you can generalize to k-way splits. 3-merge sort splits into 3 parts, still achieving O(n log n) time. The key insight is that merging k sorted arrays still takes linear time.

points: Find contiguous subarray with largest sum
Brute force: O(n³) checking all subarrays
Improved: O(n²) with precomputed sums
Divide & conquer: O(n log n) solution
Optimal: O(n) linear time algorithm exists

content.question: Which function grows fastest asymptotically?

content.text: Asymptotic analysis helps predict how algorithms scale with large inputs. A O(n²) algorithm might work fine for 100 elements but become unusable for 10,000. Understanding growth rates helps choose the right algorithm for your problem size.

content.question: What does Θ(n²) mean?
content.answer: The function grows exactly like n² - it's both O(n²) and Ω(n²). It gives a tight bound on the growth rate.

points: i doubles each iteration: O(log n) total iterations
Nested loop with outer O(n), inner O(log n): O(n log n)
Three nested linear loops: O(n³)
Loop that multiplies by 5: still O(log n)

content.text: Big O notation describes how an algorithm's running time grows as input size increases. It focuses on the dominant term and ignores constants - so 3n² + 100n becomes O(n²). This helps us compare algorithm efficiency and predict performance on large inputs.

points: O-notation: Upper bound - algorithm runs no worse than this
Ω-notation: Lower bound - algorithm takes at least this much time
Θ-notation: Tight bound - algorithm's exact growth rate
Used to classify algorithms by their worst-case, best-case, or average performance

content.text: Algorithm complexities from fastest to slowest: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(n³) cubic, O(2ⁿ) exponential. Each step up means dramatically slower performance on large inputs.

content.question: What's the time complexity of a loop that starts at i=1 and doubles i each iteration until i>n?
content.answer: O(log n) - the loop runs log₂(n) times since we're doubling i each time until it exceeds n.

points: Standard merge sort splits array into 2 parts recursively
3-merge sort splits into 3 parts instead of 2
Both have O(n log n) time complexity
k-merge sort can split into k parts but doesn't improve asymptotic complexity
Linear time merging is possible for any number of sorted arrays

content.text: The doubling hypothesis helps estimate algorithm complexity by observing how runtime changes when input size doubles. For O(n²) algorithms, doubling input makes it 4x slower. For O(n³), it's 8x slower. This empirical approach helps identify the growth rate of unknown algorithms.

content.question: Which function grows fastest asymptotically?

points: Find contiguous subarray with largest sum in an array of positive and negative integers
Brute force approach: O(n³) by checking all possible subarrays
Improved approach: O(n²) using prefix sums for constant-time subarray sum calculation
Divide and conquer: O(n log n) by splitting array and considering cross-boundary subarrays
Optimal solution: O(n) using dynamic programming (Kadane's algorithm)

content.text: Key asymptotic properties include: max(f(n), g(n)) = Θ(f(n) + g(n)), meaning the maximum of two functions grows at the same rate as their sum. Also, log(n!) = Θ(n log n), which is useful for analyzing algorithms involving permutations or factorials.

content.question: What does Θ(n²) mean for an algorithm?
content.answer: The algorithm's running time grows exactly quadratically with input size - it's both O(n²) and Ω(n²), meaning n² is both an upper and lower bound.

content.question: What's the complexity of nested loops where outer runs n times and inner doubles from 1 to n?

content.text: When analyzing nested loops, multiply their complexities. An outer loop running n times with an inner loop that doubles each iteration (log n times) gives O(n log n). Always identify the pattern of each loop level and combine appropriately.

Directed graphs (digraphs) are mathematical structures with vertices connected by edges that have direction. Unlike undirected graphs, you can only travel along edges in one specific direction, making them perfect for modeling relationships like dependencies, hierarchies, or flows.

points: Adjacency lists: Each vertex stores a list of its neighbors
Adjacency matrices: 2D array where entry (i,j) = 1 if edge exists from i to j
Adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs
Adjacency matrices allow O(1) edge lookup but use O(V²) space

content.text: A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) is a directed graph with no cycles - you can't start from a vertex and follow edges to return to the same vertex. DAGs are crucial for representing dependencies, scheduling problems, and data flow where circular dependencies would cause issues.

points: Linear ordering of vertices where each directed edge goes from earlier to later vertex
Only possible in DAGs (graphs with no cycles)
Used for scheduling tasks with dependencies
Can be computed using DFS or by repeatedly removing vertices with in-degree 0
Multiple valid topological orderings can exist for the same DAG

content.question: What is a strongly connected component in a directed graph?
content.answer: A maximal set of vertices where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex in the set via directed paths.

content.text: DFS (Depth-First Search) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, while BFS (Breadth-First Search) explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper. Both are fundamental for graph analysis and have O(V+E) time complexity.

points: Course prerequisites can be modeled as DAGs for scheduling
Snakes and ladders game states form a graph for shortest path problems
Social networks use graphs to represent relationships
Dependency resolution in software uses topological sorting

content.question: How many strongly connected components does a DAG have?
options: Always equal to number of vertices
Always equal to number of edges
Depends on the specific graph structure

content.text: A Hamiltonian path visits every vertex exactly once. In DAGs, finding Hamiltonian paths is easier than in general graphs because the acyclic property eliminates many complications, though it's still a challenging computational problem.

points: Model the problem as vertices and edges
Determine if the graph is directed or undirected
Check for cycles to identify if it's a DAG
Choose appropriate algorithms (BFS for shortest paths, DFS for connectivity)
Consider using topological sorting for dependency problems

content.text: A directed graph is a set of vertices connected by directed edges, where each edge has a specific direction from one vertex to another. Think of it like a road network where vertices are intersections and edges are one-way roads - you can only travel in the direction the arrow points.

points: Web crawling: vertices are webpages, edges are hyperlinks between pages
Garbage collection: vertices are objects, edges are pointers/references to find reachable objects
Task scheduling: vertices are jobs, edges show dependency relationships
Automata: vertices are states, edges represent state transitions for pattern matching

content.text: In directed graphs, each vertex has an in-degree (number of incoming edges) and out-degree (number of outgoing edges). A key property is that the sum of all in-degrees equals the sum of all out-degrees, which equals the total number of edges, since every edge has exactly one start and one end vertex.

points: Uses a 2D n×n array where A[i,j] = 1 if vertex i points to vertex j
Space complexity: O(n²) - can be wasteful for sparse graphs
POINTSTO operations run in O(1) time - very fast lookups
NEIGHBORS operations take O(n) time - must scan entire row
Best for dense graphs where fast edge lookups are critical
Each vertex maintains a linked list of vertices it points to
Space complexity: O(n + m) - much more efficient for sparse graphs
All operations (POINTSTO, NEIGHBORS, INSERT) take O(degree) time
Generally preferred representation for most graph algorithms
Space usage scales with actual number of edges, not theoretical maximum

content.question: What are the two main search algorithms for directed graphs and what's their time complexity?
content.answer: Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS). Both run in O(n + m) time, where n is vertices and m is edges.

content.text: Topological sorting arranges vertices in a linear order so that all directed edges point from left to right. It's only possible in directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). The algorithm repeatedly finds vertices with in-degree 0, outputs them, removes them from the graph, and repeats until done.

points: Maintain an in-degree count for each vertex and a list of zero in-degree vertices
Repeatedly remove a vertex with in-degree 0 and add it to the sorted order
When removing a vertex, decrease in-degree of all its neighbors
If any neighbor reaches in-degree 0, add it to the zero-degree list
Total time complexity: O(n + m) with proper implementation

content.question: What is the relationship between directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) and topological sorting?
options: Only some DAGs can be topologically sorted
A graph is a DAG if and only if it has a topological sorting
Topological sorting creates cycles in graphs

content.text: Two vertices are strongly connected if there's a path from each to the other. A strongly connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is strongly connected. These components can be computed in O(n + m) time using advanced algorithms.

Implicit graphs represent massive graphs without storing all vertices and edges explicitly. Instead, they use a start vertex and an algorithm to generate neighbors on demand. This is essential for applications like game AI and puzzle solving, where the full graph would be too large to store in memory.

subject: Peak Finding & Intro
title: What is a Peak in an Array?

content.text: A peak in an array is an element that is greater than or equal to its neighbors. For example, in array [2, 1, 3, 7, 3], the element 7 is a peak because it's ≥ both 3 (left neighbor) and 3 (right neighbor). Every array is guaranteed to have at least one peak.

points: Linear algorithms: Check every element sequentially, taking O(n) time
Recursive algorithm: Uses divide-and-conquer approach, taking O(log n) time
2D peak finding: Can be solved in O(n²), O(n log n), or even O(n) time depending on approach
Recursive approach is more efficient for large arrays

content.text: A valley problem is the opposite of peak finding - you're looking for an element that is smaller than or equal to its neighbors. This creates an analogous algorithmic challenge where similar divide-and-conquer techniques can be applied to find valleys efficiently.

points: Time complexity: How long the algorithm takes to run
Space complexity: How much memory the algorithm uses
Network complexity: Amount of data transmitted in distributed systems
Energy complexity: Power consumption of the algorithm
Communication complexity: Number of messages exchanged between processes

content.question: What is the maximum number of peaks possible in an array of length n?
content.answer: The maximum number of peaks is n. This occurs when the array alternates between high and low values, like [1, 2, 1, 2, 1], where every high value is a peak.

content.text: The ARRAYFUN algorithm checks if there exist two elements in an array that sum to zero (i.e., one is the negative of the other). It uses nested loops to compare every possible pair of elements, making it an O(n²) brute force solution.

points: A 2D peak must be ≥ all four neighbors (North, East, South, West)
Simple O(n²) approach: check every element in the matrix
O(n log n) solution: find max in center column, then recurse on promising half
O(n) solution exists using quadrant division technique

content.question: What is the time complexity of the recursive peak-finding algorithm?

content.text: Some problems require optimal solutions (like financial transactions or safety-critical systems), while others can use approximate solutions (like image compression or recommendation systems). The choice depends on whether small errors are acceptable or catastrophic.

points: Always check array bounds to avoid out-of-bounds errors
Linear algorithms can start from either end of the array
Recursive algorithm needs careful base case handling
Test with edge cases like single elements and identical elements

content.question: If peaks must be strictly larger than neighbors (not equal), how does this affect the recursive algorithm?
content.answer: The algorithm still works and finds peaks in O(log n) time, but now some arrays might have no peaks at all (like arrays with all identical elements).

content.question: In the array [2, 1, 3, 7, 3, 11, 1, 5, 7, 10], which elements are peaks?

content.text: In array peak finding, a peak is an element that is greater than or equal to its neighbors. For example, in array A[i], element A[i] is a peak if A[i] ≥ A[i-1] and A[i] ≥ A[i+1]. Every array of length n ≥ 1 is guaranteed to have at least one peak, making this a fundamental algorithmic problem.

points: Linear time algorithms scan through the array sequentially to find peaks
Recursive divide-and-conquer algorithm can find peaks in O(log n) time
The recursive approach checks the middle element and recurses on the side with a larger neighbor
All peak finding algorithms are guaranteed to find at least one peak in any non-empty array

content.text: The valley problem is analogous to peak finding but searches for local minima instead. A valley at position i means A[i] ≤ A[i-1] and A[i] ≤ A[i+1], where the element is smaller than or equal to its neighbors. Similar algorithmic approaches can be adapted to solve the valley finding problem.

content.question: What is the maximum number of peaks possible in an array of length n?
content.answer: The maximum number of peaks is n. This occurs when the array alternates between high and low values, making every element a peak (e.g., [5,1,5,1,5]).

points: Time complexity: how execution time grows with input size
Space complexity: how memory usage scales with input size
Communication complexity: amount of data exchanged between processes
Energy complexity: power consumption required for computation
Parallelization complexity: how well algorithm scales across multiple processors

content.text: In a 2D matrix, a peak M[i,j] must be greater than or equal to all four neighbors (North, East, South, West). A simple O(n²) algorithm checks every element, while more sophisticated divide-and-conquer approaches can achieve O(n log n) or even O(n) time complexity using clever recursive strategies.

content.question: What does the ARRAYFUN algorithm compute when it checks if A[i] + A[j] = 0 for all pairs?
options: Whether any two elements sum to zero
The maximum sum of two elements

content.text: To prove the recursive peak finding algorithm always works, we use mathematical induction with an invariant. The invariant states that in each recursive call, the subarray being examined is guaranteed to contain at least one peak, ensuring the algorithm will eventually find one.

points: Optimal solutions required: flight scheduling, medical dosage calculations, financial trading systems
Approximate solutions acceptable: image compression, weather prediction, recommendation systems
Peak finding applications: signal processing, image analysis, finding local maxima in data
Algorithm choice depends on accuracy requirements vs. computational constraints

content.question: If we change peak definition to 'strictly larger than neighbors', what happens to the recursive O(log n) algorithm?
content.answer: The algorithm can still work in O(log n) time, but arrays might not always have peaks under the strict definition (e.g., [1,1,1] has no strictly larger peaks).

content.text: When study material appears empty or incomplete, it's important to identify what information is missing and seek alternative sources. This situation often occurs with corrupted files, incomplete downloads, or formatting issues that prevent content from displaying properly.

points: Check if the original source file is corrupted or incomplete
Verify your internet connection and try reloading the content
Contact instructors or peers for alternative versions of the material
Use backup study resources like textbooks or online databases

content.question: What should you do first when encountering empty or missing study materials?
content.answer: Check if the source file is corrupted, verify your connection, and try reloading the content before seeking alternative sources.

content.text: A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure where elements are added and removed from the same end called the top. The two main operations are PUSH (add element) and POP (remove top element). Think of it like a stack of plates - you can only add or remove from the top.

A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure where elements are added at one end (rear) and removed from the other end (front). The main operations are ENQUEUE (add element) and DEQUEUE (remove element). It works like a line at a store - first person in line is first to be served.

points: Each node contains data and a reference to the next node
To delete a node: set previous node's next pointer to skip the target node
To insert a node: create new node and update pointer references
Traversal requires following next pointers from head to null
No random access - must traverse sequentially to reach elements

content.text: Doubly linked lists extend regular linked lists by adding a 'prev' reference to each node, pointing to the previous element. This allows bidirectional traversal and enables constant-time deletion of any node without needing to traverse from the head to find the previous node.

content.question: What is the key difference between how stacks and queues handle elements?
content.answer: Stacks use LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) - the most recently added element is removed first. Queues use FIFO (First-In-First-Out) - the oldest element is removed first.

points: Automatically resizes when capacity is exceeded
Provides O(1) amortized time for insertions
Uses linear space relative to current number of elements
Can implement stacks efficiently with push/pop operations
Avoids fixed-size limitations of static arrays

content.text: To insert a new node after an existing node x: first set the new node's next pointer to x.next, then update x.next to point to the new node. The order matters - if you update x.next first, you lose the reference to the rest of the list.

content.question: After performing PUSH(5), PUSH(3), POP, PUSH(7) on an empty stack, what element is at the top?

content.text: Binary search doesn't work efficiently on linked lists because they lack random access to elements. You can't jump to the middle element in O(1) time like with arrays - you must traverse sequentially, making binary search's time advantage disappear.

points: Place first stack at the beginning of array (grows rightward)
Place second stack at the end of array (grows leftward)
Overflow occurs only when stacks meet in the middle
Both PUSH and POP operations remain O(1) time
Maximizes space utilization compared to splitting array in half

content.text: You can implement a queue using two stacks by designating one for input and one for output. ENQUEUE always pushes to the input stack. DEQUEUE pops from output stack, but if output is empty, transfer all elements from input to output first.

content.question: How can you reverse a linked list in O(n) time with O(1) extra space?
content.answer: Use three pointers: previous, current, and next. Traverse the list, reversing each node's next pointer to point to the previous node, while carefully updating the three pointers to avoid losing references.

content.text: A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure where elements are added and removed from the same end called the top. The two main operations are PUSH (add element to top) and POP (remove element from top). Think of it like a stack of plates - you can only add or remove from the top.

A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure where elements are added at one end (rear) and removed from the other end (front). The main operations are ENQUEUE (add to rear) and DEQUEUE (remove from front). It works like a line at a store - first person in line is first to be served.

points: x.next = x.next.next removes the element after x from the list
To insert element t after x: set t.next = x.next, then x.next = t
Order matters - swapping insertion statements can break the list structure
Linked lists allow dynamic memory allocation without fixed size limits

content.question: What does the algorithm FOO(head) compute when given a linked list?
content.answer: FOO computes the length (number of elements) in the linked list by traversing from head to null and counting each node.

points: Stacks can be implemented with arrays or linked lists using PUSH/POP operations
Queues can be implemented with arrays or linked lists using ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE operations
Two stacks can share a single array by growing from opposite ends
A queue can be implemented using two stacks with careful operation management

content.text: Dynamic arrays automatically resize when they run out of space, allowing unlimited growth without declaring a fixed size. When implementing stacks with dynamic arrays, both PUSH and POP operations can achieve O(1) amortized time complexity while maintaining linear space usage relative to current elements.

content.question: What sequence results from: PUSH(4), PUSH(1), PUSH(3), POP, PUSH(8), POP on an empty stack?

content.text: To insert into a sorted linked list, traverse from the head until you find the correct position, then insert the new element while maintaining sorted order. Binary search won't work efficiently on linked lists because you can't jump to the middle element - you must traverse sequentially.

points: Reverse a linked list in Θ(n) time using constant extra space
Use three pointers: previous, current, and next
Traverse the list once, reversing the next pointers as you go
The original head becomes the new tail, original tail becomes new head

content.text: Algorithm BAR(x, s) is a recursive function that computes the sum of all values in a linked list starting from node x, adding them to the accumulator s. It uses tail recursion to traverse the list and accumulate the sum of all node keys.

Graphs are data structures consisting of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. They're used to model relationships between objects, like social networks, road maps, or computer networks. Graphs can be represented using adjacency lists or adjacency matrices.

points: Adjacency List: Each vertex stores a list of its neighbors
Adjacency Matrix: 2D array where entry (i,j) is 1 if edge exists between vertices i and j
Adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs
Adjacency matrices allow O(1) edge lookup time
Choice depends on graph density and operations needed

content.text: DFS explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses recursion or an explicit stack to keep track of vertices. DFS produces a tree structure and assigns discovery/finish times to each vertex.

BFS explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbors of a vertex before moving to the next level. It uses a queue data structure and finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. BFS calculates the minimum distance from the starting vertex to all other vertices.

points: A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path
In undirected graphs, you can find components using DFS or BFS
Each vertex belongs to exactly one connected component
A graph with one component is called connected
Disconnected graphs have multiple components

content.question: What is a bipartite graph?
content.answer: A graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. All edges connect vertices from different sets.

content.text: A cycle in a graph is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex. You can detect cycles using DFS by checking if you encounter a vertex that's already been visited in the current path. This is fundamental for determining if a graph is acyclic.

points: Vertices arranged in rows and columns like a grid
Only horizontally and vertically adjacent vertices can be connected
A k×k grid graph has k² vertices
Commonly used to model mazes and 2D spatial problems
Edges connect only neighboring cells in 4 directions

content.text: An Euler tour visits every edge exactly once and returns to the starting vertex. An Euler path visits every edge exactly once but doesn't need to return to start. A graph has an Euler tour if and only if all vertices have even degree.

content.question: What is the time complexity of BFS and DFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges?

content.text: The diameter of a tree is the longest shortest path between any two vertices in the tree. Since trees have no cycles, there's exactly one path between any two vertices. Computing diameter helps understand the tree's "width" or maximum distance.

content.question: How can you implement DFS without using recursion?
content.answer: Use an explicit stack data structure. Push the starting vertex onto the stack, then repeatedly pop a vertex, mark it as visited, and push all its unvisited neighbors onto the stack.

content.text: An undirected graph is a set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. Graphs model countless real-world problems like computer networks, transportation systems, social media connections, and molecular structures. They're fundamental data structures with hundreds of well-known algorithms.

points: V = set of vertices, E = set of edges, where each edge connects two vertices
Path: sequence of vertices connected by edges
Cycle: path that starts and ends at the same vertex
Degree of vertex v: number of neighbors or edges connected to v
Two vertices are connected if there's a path between them

content.text: In any undirected graph, the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges (∑ deg(v) = 2m). This works because each edge contributes 1 to the degree of both vertices it connects, so every edge is counted exactly twice in the total.

points: Internet: computers as vertices, cables as edges
Transportation: intersections/airports as vertices, roads/flights as edges
Social networks: people as vertices, friendships as edges
Biology: proteins as vertices, interactions as edges
Financial: stocks as vertices, transactions as edges
Path finding: Is there a path between two vertices?
Shortest path: What's the minimum distance between vertices?
Cycle detection: Does the graph contain any cycles?
Connectivity: Are all vertices reachable from each other?
Hamilton cycle: Is there a cycle visiting each vertex exactly once?

content.text: Graphs need efficient data structures supporting key operations: checking if vertices are adjacent, finding all neighbors of a vertex, and inserting new edges. The choice of representation affects the time and space complexity of these operations.

points: 2D array A where A[i,j] = 1 if vertices i and j are connected
Space complexity: O(n²) where n is number of vertices
ADJACENT and INSERT operations: O(1) time
NEIGHBORS operation: O(n) time
Good for dense graphs with many edges

content.question: In graph notation G = (V, E), what do V and E represent?
content.answer: V is the set of vertices (nodes) and E is the set of edges (connections between vertices)

content.question: If a graph has 5 vertices and the sum of all degrees is 12, how many edges does it have?

content.text: Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) are fundamental algorithms for exploring graphs. DFS goes as deep as possible before backtracking, while BFS explores all neighbors at the current level before moving deeper. Both are essential for solving connectivity and pathfinding problems.

subject: Shortest Paths & MST
title: What is a Minimum Spanning Tree?

content.text: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is a subset of edges in a weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum total weight possible, without forming any cycles. It's like finding the cheapest way to connect all points in a network while ensuring everything stays connected.

points: Sort all edges by weight from lightest to heaviest
Start with an empty set of edges
For each edge, add it to the MST if it doesn't create a cycle
Stop when you have n-1 edges (where n is the number of vertices)
Use a union-find data structure to efficiently detect cycles
Start from any vertex and add it to the MST
Use a priority queue to track edges from MST vertices to non-MST vertices
Always pick the lightest edge that connects an MST vertex to a non-MST vertex
Add the new vertex and edge to the MST
Repeat until all vertices are included

content.text: The lightest edge in any graph is always part of some MST, but the heaviest edge may or may not be included. If all edge weights are distinct, there's exactly one unique MST. Scaling all weights by the same positive factor doesn't change which edges form the MST.

content.question: How does the reverse deletion algorithm work to find an MST?
content.answer: Start with all edges, then examine edges from heaviest to lightest. Remove an edge only if its removal doesn't disconnect the graph. The remaining edges form an MST.

content.text: The cut property states that the lightest edge crossing any cut must be in some MST. The cycle property states that the heaviest edge in any cycle cannot be in an MST. These properties work even when edge weights aren't distinct, and they justify why Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms are correct.

points: Kruskal's: Sort edges globally, use union-find, good for sparse graphs
Prim's: Grow tree locally, use priority queue, good for dense graphs
Both have O(E log E) time complexity for most implementations
Reverse deletion: Start with all edges, remove heaviest that don't disconnect
All three algorithms produce correct MSTs

content.question: Which real-world problem is best solved using a Minimum Spanning Tree?
options: Finding shortest path between two cities
Connecting buildings with minimum cable cost
Finding the maximum flow in a network

content.text: To find a Maximum Spanning Tree (tree with maximum total weight), you can transform the problem by negating all edge weights, finding the MST of the transformed graph, then converting back. Alternatively, modify existing MST algorithms to pick heaviest edges instead of lightest ones.

content.question: How can you efficiently compute an MST when all edge weights are small integers like {1, 2, ..., 10}?
content.answer: Use counting sort or bucket sort to sort edges in O(m) time instead of O(m log m), making the overall algorithm faster since sorting is often the bottleneck.

content.text: When all edge weights in a graph are distinct, there is exactly one unique MST. However, when weights can be equal, multiple MSTs may exist. Kruskal's algorithm can return different valid MSTs depending on how ties are broken when sorting edges with equal weights.

subject: Shortest Paths & MST
title: What is a Minimum Spanning Tree?

content.text: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is a subgraph that connects all vertices in a weighted graph with the minimum total edge weight possible. It must be connected (all vertices reachable) and acyclic (no cycles), using exactly n-1 edges for n vertices.

points: Weighted graph: Each edge has a numerical weight w(e)
Spanning tree: Connected and acyclic subgraph covering all vertices
MST: Spanning tree with minimum total weight
Uses exactly n-1 edges for n vertices

content.text: Minimum spanning trees have wide applications in network design (computer networks, roads, electrical circuits), approximation algorithms for problems like traveling salesperson, and fields like meteorology, biomedical analysis, and image processing.

points: A cut partitions vertices into two non-empty sets
Cut edges cross between the two sets
The lightest cut edge is always in the MST
This property helps prove correctness of MST algorithms

content.text: For any cycle in a graph, the heaviest edge in that cycle cannot be part of the minimum spanning tree. This is because we could replace it with a lighter edge to get a spanning tree with smaller total weight.

points: Start from any vertex s
Grow tree T by adding lightest edge with one endpoint in T
Use priority queue to efficiently find lightest cut edge
Stop when T has n-1 edges
Time complexity: O(m log n) with binary heap

content.text: Prim's algorithm grows the MST one vertex at a time, always adding the lightest edge that connects a vertex in the current tree to a vertex outside it. It uses the cut property to ensure the result is optimal.

points: Sort all edges by weight from lightest to heaviest
Consider edges in sorted order
Add edge to tree if it doesn't create a cycle
Use dynamic connectivity to check for cycles
Time complexity: O(m log n)

content.question: What are the two key properties used to prove MST algorithm correctness?
content.answer: Cut Property (lightest cut edge is in MST) and Cycle Property (heaviest cycle edge is not in MST)

content.text: Prim's algorithm grows the tree vertex by vertex from a starting point, while Kruskal's considers edges globally by weight. Both are greedy algorithms with O(m log n) time complexity, but use different approaches to build the MST.

points: Array: O(n²) total time, simple implementation
Binary heap: O(m log n) total time, good general choice
Fibonacci heap: O(m + n log n) total time, best theoretical
Choice depends on graph density and implementation complexity

content.question: Which algorithm considers edges in order from lightest to heaviest?

content.text: Range queries calculate values from subarrays of an array, like finding the sum, minimum, or maximum in a specific range. While a simple loop approach works in O(n) time per query, specialized data structures can answer these queries much more efficiently in O(1) or O(log n) time.

points: sumq(a, b): calculate sum of values in range [a, b]
minq(a, b): find minimum value in range [a, b]
maxq(a, b): find maximum value in range [a, b]
Each query operates on a subarray between two indices

content.text: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums where each position k contains sum(0, k) from the original array. Once built in O(n) time, any range sum can be calculated in O(1) time using the formula: sum(a, b) = sum(0, b) - sum(0, a-1).

points: Also called Binary Indexed Tree (BIT)
Supports range sum queries in O(log n) time
Allows array updates in O(log n) time
Uses bit operations like k&-k for efficient implementation
Dynamic alternative to static prefix sum arrays

content.text: Segment trees are binary trees that support both range queries and updates in O(log n) time. Unlike Fenwick trees which only handle sum queries, segment trees can process minimum, maximum, and other types of range queries, making them more versatile but requiring more memory.

content.question: What is the time complexity of answering a range sum query using a prefix sum array?
content.answer: O(1) - constant time, because it only requires two array lookups and one subtraction operation.

points: Requires O(n log n) preprocessing time
Precalculates values for ranges with power-of-two lengths
Answers queries in O(1) time after preprocessing
Uses sparse table method with overlapping ranges
More complex than sum queries but still efficient

content.text: Static range queries work on arrays that never change, allowing simple preprocessing with prefix sums. Dynamic queries need to handle array updates between queries, requiring more sophisticated data structures like Fenwick trees or segment trees that maintain efficiency during modifications.

content.question: Which data structure is best for handling both range sum queries and frequent array updates?

points: Binary tree with array elements at bottom level
Internal nodes store information for range queries
Each range can be divided into O(log n) tree nodes
Updates require modifying O(log n) nodes along a path
Stored as array with 2n elements for n-sized input

content.text: A priority queue is a data structure where elements are served based on their priority rather than insertion order. The highest priority element is always removed first, making it perfect for task scheduling and resource allocation problems.

points: A heap is a complete binary tree where parent nodes have higher (max-heap) or lower (min-heap) values than their children
Can be efficiently represented as an array where element at index i has children at 2i and 2i+1
Maintains the heap property: every parent satisfies the ordering constraint with its children
Height is O(log n) which enables efficient operations

content.text: While a sorted array satisfies heap property (each parent is larger than children), it's not the optimal heap representation. Heaps prioritize fast insertion and extraction over complete ordering, making them better for priority queue operations.

points: INSERT: Add new element while maintaining heap property in O(log n) time
EXTRACTMAX: Remove and return largest element in O(log n) time
INCREASEKEY: Increase an element's priority and restore heap property in O(log n) time
All operations preserve the fundamental heap structure

content.question: Where can the minimum element be found in a max-heap?
content.answer: The minimum element in a max-heap can only be found in the leaf nodes (bottom level), since all internal nodes must be larger than their children.

content.text: A complete binary tree of height h has exactly 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes. This can be calculated as 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^h, which forms a geometric series that sums to this formula.

points: REMOVELARGEST(m): Remove m largest elements in O(m log n) time
DELETE(x): Remove specific element x in O(log n) time
FUSION(x,y): Combine two elements into one with sum of their keys in O(log n) time
THRESHOLD(k): Return all elements with key ≥ k in O(m) time where m is result size

content.text: Heaps can store additional information (satellite data) like age, weight, or other attributes alongside the key. This extra data can be accessed in O(1) time using the element's index without affecting standard heap operation performance.

content.question: In a heap represented as an array starting at index 1, where are the children of element at index i located?

content.text: You can merge k sorted arrays containing n total elements into a single sorted array in O(n log k) time using a min-heap. Keep the smallest element from each array in the heap and repeatedly extract the minimum.

points: Use a max-heap to always access the most difficult available task
NEWTASK(i,d): Insert task with id i and difficulty d into heap
REQUESTTASK(): Extract and return the task id with highest difficulty
Perfect real-world example of priority queue application

content.question: What is the time complexity of INSERT, EXTRACTMAX, and INCREASEKEY operations in a heap?
content.answer: All three operations run in O(log n) time, where n is the number of elements in the heap, because they may need to traverse the height of the tree.

content.text: A priority queue is a data structure that maintains a dynamic set where each element has a key and supports operations like MAX() to find the largest element, EXTRACTMAX() to remove it, INSERT() to add new elements, and INCREASEKEY() to update values. It's like a regular queue, but elements are served based on priority rather than arrival order.

points: MAX(): return element with largest key
EXTRACTMAX(): return and remove element with largest key
INCREASEKEY(x, k): set x.key = k (assumes k ≥ x.key)
INSERT(x): add element x to the set S
Scheduling tasks by priority
Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths
Prim's algorithm for minimum spanning trees
Huffman's algorithm for compression
Any scenario where you need the 'most important' item quickly

content.text: A heap is an almost complete binary tree that satisfies heap-order property: for any node, all keys in its left and right subtrees are less than or equal to the node's key. This structure allows efficient priority queue operations while maintaining a compact tree shape.

points: Store heap nodes in array H[1..n] in level order
PARENT(x): return x/2
LEFT(x): return 2x
RIGHT(x): return 2x + 1
All operations run in O(1) time with O(n) space

content.text: BUBBLEUP fixes heap violations by swapping a node with its parent when the node's key is too large, continuing until heap order is restored. BUBBLEDOWN does the opposite, swapping with the larger child when a node's key is too small. Both operations take O(log n) time.

points: MAX(): O(1) - just return H[1]
EXTRACTMAX(): O(log n) - replace root with last element, then bubble down
INSERT(): O(log n) - add at end, then bubble up
INCREASEKEY(): O(log n) - update value, then bubble up

content.question: What's the difference between a complete binary tree and an almost complete binary tree?
content.answer: A complete binary tree has all levels completely filled. An almost complete binary tree is a complete tree with 0 or more rightmost leaves deleted from the last level.

content.text: You can build a heap from an unsorted array in O(n) time using bottom-up construction: apply BUBBLEDOWN to all nodes in decreasing level order. This is faster than the O(n log n) top-down approach that uses BUBBLEUP on each node.

points: Build a heap from the input array in O(n) time
Perform n EXTRACTMAX operations in O(n log n) time
Place extracted elements at the end of array
Results in O(n log n) total time with O(1) extra space
It's an in-place sorting algorithm

content.question: Which data structure gives the best overall performance for priority queue operations?
options: Linked list - O(n) max, O(1) insert
Sorted linked list - O(1) max, O(n) insert
Heap - O(1) max, O(log n) insert

content.question: What is the height of an almost complete binary tree with n nodes?
content.answer: Θ(log n) - this logarithmic height is what makes heap operations efficient.

content.text: Insertion sort builds a sorted array one element at a time by taking each unsorted element and inserting it into its correct position among the already sorted elements. It works similarly to how you might sort playing cards in your hand, moving each card to its proper place.

Merge sort uses a divide-and-conquer approach by recursively splitting an array into smaller subarrays until each contains only one element, then merging them back together in sorted order. This algorithm guarantees O(n log n) time complexity regardless of input data.

points: Insertion sort: O(n²) time, good for small arrays
Merge sort: O(n log n) time, stable and predictable
Quick sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case
Binary search can optimize insertion step in insertion sort

content.text: Finding duplicates in an array can be solved in O(n²) time by comparing every pair of elements, or optimized to O(n log n) by first sorting the array and then checking adjacent elements. The sorting approach is more efficient for larger datasets.

points: 2Sum: Find two numbers that add to zero
Brute force 2Sum: O(n²) by checking all pairs
Optimized 2Sum: O(n log n) using sorting and binary search
3Sum extends the concept to three numbers summing to zero

content.question: Can binary search improve insertion sort's performance, and how?
content.answer: Binary search can speed up finding the insertion position from O(n) to O(log n), but the shifting of elements still takes O(n) time, so overall complexity remains O(n²).

content.text: The rank k problem asks for the kth smallest element in an array. While sorting first gives O(n log n) solution, clever partitioning techniques can solve it in O(n) average time using methods similar to quicksort's partition step.

points: Partition separates array into elements ≤ pivot and > pivot
QuickSort uses recursive partitioning around pivot elements
Median makes a good pivot for balanced partitions
Partition step runs in O(n) linear time

content.question: What is the time complexity of the optimized 3Sum algorithm?

content.text: To maximize the number of stones Josefine can carry within weight limit W, use a greedy approach: sort stones by weight and keep adding the lightest stones until adding another would exceed the weight capacity.

points: Divide and conquer: Split problem into smaller subproblems
Greedy: Make locally optimal choices at each step
Binary search: Reduce search space by half each iteration
Sorting as preprocessing: Often enables faster subsequent operations

subject: Search Trees
title: What is a Binary Search Tree?

content.text: A binary search tree (BST) is a tree data structure where each node has at most two children, and for every node, all values in the left subtree are smaller than the node's value, while all values in the right subtree are larger. This ordering property makes searching, insertion, and deletion operations efficient.

points: Left subtree contains only values smaller than the current node
Right subtree contains only values larger than the current node
Both left and right subtrees are also binary search trees
The smallest element is always the leftmost node
The largest element is always the rightmost node

content.text: There are three main ways to traverse a binary tree: preorder (visit root, then left, then right), inorder (visit left, then root, then right), and postorder (visit left, then right, then root). In a BST, inorder traversal visits nodes in sorted order from smallest to largest.

content.question: Where are the smallest and largest elements located in a binary search tree?
content.answer: The smallest element is at the leftmost node (keep going left until you can't), and the largest element is at the rightmost node (keep going right until you can't).

content.text: A 2-3 tree is a balanced search tree where each internal node has either 2 or 3 children, and all leaves are at the same level. Each node can store 1 or 2 keys, maintaining sorted order. This structure guarantees O(log n) height, making all operations efficient.

points: All leaves are at the same level (perfectly balanced)
Internal nodes have 2 or 3 children
Nodes contain 1 or 2 keys in sorted order
Height is always O(log n)
Automatically stays balanced during insertions and deletions

content.text: Binary search trees maintain left < root < right ordering for efficient searching, while heaps maintain parent ≥ children (max-heap) or parent ≤ children (min-heap) for efficient priority operations. BSTs optimize for search/sort operations, while heaps optimize for finding min/max elements.

points: PREDECESSOR(k): Find largest element ≤ k
RANGEREPORT(k1,k2): Return all elements in range [k1,k2]
RANGECOUNT(k1,k2): Count elements in range [k1,k2]
All can be implemented in O(log n) time on balanced trees

content.question: Which traversal method visits nodes in sorted order in a binary search tree?

content.text: The height of a binary search tree affects performance - balanced trees have O(log n) height giving efficient operations, while unbalanced trees can degrade to O(n) height in worst case. Balanced trees like 2-3 trees automatically maintain optimal height through self-balancing operations.

points: Minimum height for n nodes: ⌊log₂ n⌋
Maximum height for n nodes: n-1 (degenerate case)
2-3 trees guarantee height is Θ(log n)
Height directly impacts operation time complexity
Balanced trees maintain optimal performance

content.text: Dynamic ordered sets maintain a collection of elements with unique keys, supporting three core operations: SEARCH(k) to find an element, INSERT(x) to add a new element, and DELETE(x) to remove an element. The key challenge is efficiently supporting these operations while keeping elements ordered by their keys.

points: Each node has at most two children (left and right)
Maintains symmetric order: left subtree ≤ node.key ≤ right subtree
Inorder traversal visits nodes in sorted key order
Operations take O(h) time where h is tree height
Height can be Ω(n) worst-case, Θ(log n) on average

content.text: To search for a key k in a binary search tree, start at the root and compare k with the current node's key. If equal, return the element. If k is smaller, go left; if larger, go right. If you reach a null pointer, the key doesn't exist.

points: 0 children: Simply remove the node
1 child: Replace node with its single child
2 children: Find successor (smallest key in right subtree), replace node's key with successor's key, then delete successor
All cases run in O(h) time

content.question: What order does inorder traversal visit nodes in a binary search tree?
content.answer: Sorted order by key values (ascending)

content.text: 2-3 trees maintain perfect balance by ensuring every path from root to leaf has the same length. Each internal node has either 2 children with 1 key, or 3 children with 2 keys. This guarantees O(log n) height and operations.

points: Linked list: O(n) search, O(1) insert/delete
Sorted array: O(log n) search, O(n) insert/delete
Binary search tree: O(h) for all operations
2-3 tree: O(log n) for all operations
Space complexity is O(n) for all structures

content.text: To insert into a 2-3 tree, first search to find the insertion point at a leaf. Add the new key, and if the node becomes too large (more than 2 keys), move the middle key up to the parent and split the node. Repeat this process up the tree if necessary.

content.question: In a binary search tree with symmetric order, where are keys greater than the current node located?
What is the worst-case time complexity for BST operations when the tree is completely unbalanced?
content.answer: O(n) because the height h can be equal to n in a linear chain

content.text: While basic binary search trees can degrade to O(n) height in worst case, balanced search trees like 2-3 trees maintain O(log n) height through structural constraints. This guarantees efficient O(log n) operations regardless of insertion order.

Range Minimum Queries (RMQ) are a fundamental problem in computer science where we need to efficiently find the minimum element in any given range of an array. This problem appears frequently in competitive programming and real-world applications, requiring specialized data structures like segment trees to achieve optimal performance.

points: A segment tree is a binary tree data structure used for storing intervals or segments
Each leaf represents a single array element, while internal nodes represent merged information from their children
Common operations include range queries (sum, min, max) and point updates
Time complexity is O(log n) for both queries and updates
Space complexity is O(n) with the tree stored in an array format

content.text: Segment trees support two main operations: ADD(index, value) which updates a single element, and range queries that retrieve aggregated information over a range. The tree structure allows these operations to run in O(log n) time by only visiting nodes along the path from root to leaf, making it much faster than naive O(n) approaches.

content.question: What is the time complexity for both query and update operations in a segment tree?
content.answer: O(log n) - because we only need to traverse from root to leaf, visiting at most log n nodes in the tree structure.

points: Range sum queries find the total sum of elements in a given range [i, j]
Can be solved using segment trees, prefix sums, or Fenwick trees
Segment trees allow both range queries and point updates in O(log n) time
Prefix sums provide O(1) queries but O(n) updates
Useful for problems involving cumulative data analysis

content.text: The range update problem involves efficiently adding a value to all elements in a range [i, j] and looking up individual elements. This requires techniques like lazy propagation in segment trees or difference arrays to avoid the naive O(n) cost of updating each element individually, achieving O(log n) performance instead.

points: Database indexing for range queries on large datasets
Geographic information systems for spatial range searches
Financial systems for calculating portfolio values over time periods
Game development for collision detection and spatial partitioning
Image processing for region-based operations and filters

content.question: In a segment tree stored as an array, where are the leaf nodes typically located?
options: At the beginning of the array
In the second half of the array

content.text: Preprocessing arrays for range queries involves building auxiliary data structures that enable fast query responses. The choice between segment trees, sparse tables, or other structures depends on whether the array is static or dynamic, and whether updates are needed alongside queries.

points: Range Minimum Query (RMQ): find the smallest element in a range
Range Maximum Query: find the largest element in a range
Range Sum Query: calculate the total sum of elements in a range
Range GCD Query: find the greatest common divisor of elements in a range
Range XOR Query: compute the XOR of all elements in a range

content.text: Range Minimum Query (RMQ) is a problem where you preprocess an array to efficiently answer queries about the minimum element in any given range. Given an array A[1...n], RMQ(i,j) returns the minimum element in the subarray from index i to j.

points: Solution 1: Store array, scan for each query - O(n) space, O(n) time
Solution 2: Precompute all answers in matrix - O(n²) space, O(1) time
Trade-off between preprocessing time/space and query speed
Matrix solution stores answer for every possible (i,j) pair

content.text: Dynamic RMQ extends the basic problem by allowing updates to array elements. It supports both RMQ(i,j) queries and Add(i,k) operations that modify A[i] = A[i] + k, where k can be negative.

points: Array solution: O(n) space, O(n) query, O(1) update
Matrix solution: O(n²) space, O(1) query, O(n²) update
Updates in matrix approach require recalculating many entries
Need better data structure for balanced performance

content.text: Segment trees provide an optimal solution for dynamic RMQ by organizing data in a binary tree structure. Each node stores the minimum value of its range, allowing both queries and updates to be performed in O(log n) time with O(n) space.

points: Space complexity: O(n) - linear in array size
Query time: O(log n) - traverse tree height
Update time: O(log n) - modify path from leaf to root
Balanced solution for dynamic range queries

content.text: To query RMQ(i,j) in a segment tree, start with pointers at positions i and j. Move inward while taking minimum values from nodes that represent complete ranges within the query interval, then move both pointers up to their parents until they meet.

When updating an element in a segment tree, you modify the leaf node and then propagate the change upward to the root. Each ancestor node may need to recalculate its minimum value based on its children, requiring O(log n) operations.

content.question: What does RMQ(i,j) return?
content.answer: The minimum element in the array range from index i to index j

content.question: What is the time complexity for both queries and updates in a segment tree?

points: Initialize s = infinity, set pointers a = i and b = j
While a is not right of b, check if nodes are complete ranges
If a is right child, take minimum and move a right
If b is left child, take minimum and move b left
Move both pointers to parent nodes and repeat

content.text: Range Minimum Query (RMQ) is a data structure problem where you preprocess an array A[1...n] to efficiently answer queries RMQ(i,j) that return the minimum element in the subarray A[i...j]. The goal is to balance preprocessing time, space usage, and query time complexity.

points: Solution 1: Store original array - O(n) space, O(n) query time
Solution 2: Precompute all queries in matrix - O(n²) space, O(1) query time
Trade-off between space and query time complexity
Static solutions don't support array updates after preprocessing

content.text: Dynamic RMQ extends the basic problem by supporting updates to the array. It must handle both RMQ(i,j) queries to find minimum values and Add(i,k) operations to modify array elements by adding k to position i (k can be negative).

points: Array solution: O(n) space, O(n) query, O(1) update time
Matrix solution: O(n²) space, O(1) query, O(n²) update time
Updates in matrix approach require recalculating many entries
Need better data structure for balanced performance

content.text: Segment trees provide an optimal solution for dynamic RMQ problems. They use O(n) space and support both queries and updates in O(log n) time by organizing data in a binary tree structure where each node stores the minimum of its subtree.

points: Space complexity: O(n) - much better than O(n²) matrix
Query time: O(log n) - logarithmic traversal of tree height
Update time: O(log n) - propagate changes up the tree path
Each internal node stores minimum of its children's ranges

content.question: What are the time and space complexities for segment trees in dynamic RMQ?
content.answer: Space: O(n), Query time: O(log n), Update time: O(log n). This provides a good balance between all operations compared to naive approaches.

content.text: To query RMQ(i,j) in a segment tree, start with pointers at positions i and j. Move inward by taking minimum values from right children of left pointer and left children of right pointer, then move both pointers up to their parents until they meet.

points: Add(i,k) operation modifies array element at position i
Update leaf node in segment tree with new value
Propagate changes upward through all ancestor nodes
Each ancestor recalculates its minimum from children

content.question: Which approach gives the best overall performance for dynamic RMQ with frequent queries and updates?

content.text: Shortest path algorithms find the path between two nodes in a graph with the minimum total weight. These algorithms are fundamental in computer science and have applications in routing, navigation, network optimization, and many other domains where finding optimal paths is crucial.

points: Works only with non-negative edge weights
Finds shortest paths from a single source to all other nodes
Uses a greedy approach with a priority queue
Time complexity is O((V + E) log V) with binary heap
Fails with negative edges because it assumes found distances are final

content.text: A shortest path tree rooted at node s contains the shortest paths from s to all reachable nodes in the graph. It's a subgraph that forms a tree structure where each node's parent represents the next step on the optimal path back to the source.

content.question: What happens when you run Dijkstra's algorithm on a graph with negative edge weights?
content.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm produces incorrect results because it assumes that once a node is processed, its shortest distance is final. Negative edges can create shorter paths that are discovered later.

points: Topologically sort the vertices of the DAG
Initialize distances: source = 0, all others = negative infinity
Process vertices in topological order
For each vertex, update distances to its neighbors
Works because DAGs have no cycles to create contradictions

content.text: To maximize signal quality in cable networks, we need shortest path algorithms since signal degrades proportionally to cable length. When boxes add extra cost (like 5 meters of cable), we modify edge weights to include this penalty before running the shortest path algorithm.

When AlgoNet wants to minimize cable maintenance costs while connecting all houses, this becomes a Minimum Spanning Tree problem. We need to find the subset of edges that connects all nodes with minimum total weight, not shortest paths.

points: Convert probabilities to logarithms to use addition instead of multiplication
Use negative log values so maximum probability becomes minimum weight
Apply shortest path algorithms on the transformed graph
Convert final result back using exponential function
Independent probabilities multiply along paths

content.question: What happens to a shortest path tree T when you add the same constant c to all edge weights in the graph?
options: T may or may not remain optimal
T becomes a maximum spanning tree

content.text: A loopy tree is created by taking a binary tree and adding edges from every leaf back to the root. This creates a special graph structure that's mostly tree-like but has some cycles, which can potentially allow for more efficient shortest path algorithms than general graphs.

points: Check if tree T is rooted at source s
Verify all edges in T have correct shortest distances
For each node, confirm no other path is shorter
Use relaxation: if dist[u] + weight(u,v) < dist[v], then T is not optimal
All tree edges must satisfy the shortest path property

content.text: The shortest paths problem asks us to find the shortest path from a source vertex s to all other vertices in a directed, weighted graph. The solution is typically represented as a shortest path tree rooted at s, which efficiently captures all shortest paths from the source.

points: Routing in computer networks and GPS navigation
Scheduling tasks with dependencies
Pipelining in computer architectures
Resource allocation and optimization problems

content.text: A key property of shortest paths is that any subpath of a shortest path is also a shortest path. This means if we have the shortest path from s to t, then every portion of that path between any two vertices on it is also shortest between those vertices.

points: Initialize source distance to 0, all others to infinity
Maintain a priority queue of unvisited vertices
Extract vertex with minimum distance estimate
Relax all outgoing edges by updating distance estimates
Repeat until all vertices are processed

content.text: Edge relaxation is the core operation in shortest path algorithms. For edge (u,v), if the path through u gives a shorter distance to v than currently known, we update v's distance to u.distance + weight(u,v). This process gradually improves distance estimates.

content.question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm using a binary heap?
content.answer: O(m log n) where m is the number of edges and n is the number of vertices, due to n extract-min operations and up to m decrease-key operations, each taking O(log n) time.

points: n INSERT operations to add all vertices initially
n EXTRACT-MIN operations to process each vertex
Up to m DECREASE-KEY operations when relaxing edges
Total complexity depends on the priority queue implementation chosen

content.text: Finding shortest paths in Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) is computationally easier than general graphs. We can process vertices in topological order and relax edges, achieving O(m + n) time complexity. This approach even works with negative edge weights.

content.question: What is a key requirement for Dijkstra's algorithm to work correctly?
options: All edge weights must be non-negative
There must be exactly n-1 edges

points: Array implementation: O(n²) total time, simple but slow
Binary heap: O(m log n) total time, good general choice
Fibonacci heap: O(m + n log n) total time, optimal but complex
Choice depends on graph density and implementation complexity

content.text: Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that always selects the closest unvisited vertex next. This greedy choice is optimal because once we select a vertex, we know its shortest path distance is final - no future relaxations can improve it.

content.question: What is the main advantage of the DAG shortest path algorithm over Dijkstra's?
options: It has O(m + n) time complexity
It doesn't need a priority queue

content.text: 02105 Algorithms and Data Structures 1 is a computer science course that uses the CLRS textbook 'Introduction to Algorithms' 4th edition. The course covers fundamental algorithms and data structures concepts through a structured weekly format combining lectures, exercise classes, and hands-on programming work.

points: 8:00-10:00 Exercise class with TAs on previous lecture material
10:00-10:45 Walkthrough of selected exercises from the class
10:50-12:00 Lecture on new material
Week 0 is the first exercise class with no corresponding lecture

content.text: Before each exercise class, you should familiarize yourself with all exercises and work on at least half of them beforehand. Don't expect to solve all exercises during class time - focus on your weak points and work in your preferred order to maximize learning.

points: [w] Warmup exercises - should be easy if you understand the material
[*] Difficult exercises requiring clever insights
[**] Very difficult exercises needing creative problem-solving
[†] Programming implementation exercises

content.text: You can use any standard imperative programming language like Python, Java, C, or C++ for implementation exercises. Python is the default programming language for the course, making it a good choice if you're unsure which language to use.

content.question: What is the main textbook used in 02105 Algorithms and Data Structures 1?
content.answer: 'Introduction to Algorithms' 4th edition by Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, and Stein (CLRS)

content.text: The course offers several voluntary hand-in exercises throughout the semester. These are non-mandatory but provide additional practice and learning opportunities. Details about these exercises can be found on the course homepage.

content.question: What happens during the 10:00-10:45 time slot in the weekly schedule?
options: Walkthrough of selected exercises from the exercise class

content.text: Union Find is a data structure that efficiently tracks connected components in a dynamic graph. It supports two main operations: UNION (connect two elements) and FIND (check if two elements are connected). This makes it perfect for problems like network connectivity and dynamic graph analysis.

points: Quick Find: Fast lookups O(1) but slow unions O(n)
Quick Union: Uses tree structure, but can create tall trees
Weighted Quick Union: Keeps trees balanced by merging smaller into larger
Path Compression: Flattens trees during FIND to speed up future operations

content.text: Quick Find uses an array where each element stores its component ID. FIND operations are O(1) since you just check the array. However, UNION operations are O(n) because you must update all elements in one component to match the other component's ID.
